1: ḥānnēnî ʾĕlōhîm kĕḥasdekā — have mercy on me, O God, according to your steadfast love. The triple ground of the petition: ḥesed (steadfast love) + raḥămîm (compassion, tender mercies — from reḥem: the womb; the visceral compassion of a mother for her child) + ḥesed again in expanded form. The three divine attributes are offered as the only basis the Psalmist can appeal to — not innocence but divine character. kĕrōb raḥămêkhā meḥē pĕshāʿay — in your great compassion, blot out my transgressions. Māḥāh (to blot out, wipe away — as ink is wiped from a tablet; the record of pĕshāʿîm (rebellious acts — the most willful category of sin, from pāshaʿ: to rebel, to transgress deliberately against authority) erased from the divine ledger.
2: harbeḥ kabbĕsênî mēʿăwōnî — scrub away my guilt completely. Kābaś (to wash by pressing, kneading, treading — the vigorous washing that requires physical effort; the laundering of heavily soiled cloth, not a gentle rinse). The intensive washing vocabulary: harbeḥ kabbĕsênî (wash me greatly — the adverb harbeḥ intensifying the already intense verb). ûmēḥaṭṭāʾtî ṭahărênî — and purify me from my sin. Ṭāhēr (piel: to declare clean, to purify — the priestly verdict of purification). Ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin — the missing of the mark, the deviation from the path).
3: kî-pĕshāʿay ʾănî ʾēdāʿ — for I know my rebellious acts. The acknowledgment of full awareness — the Psalmist cannot plead ignorance. wĕḥaṭṭāʾtî negdî tāmîd — and my sin is before me at every moment. Negdî tāmîd (before me always — continuously present to the conscience). The sin that was committed at a specific moment (the David/Bathsheba crisis) has become an unrelenting presence in the sinner's self-awareness.
4: lĕkhā lĕbaddekā ḥāṭāʾtî wĕhārāʿ bĕʿênêkhā ʿāśîtî — against you — you alone — have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight. Lĕkhā lĕbaddekā (to you, to you alone — the exclusive particle lĕbad) emphasizes the vertical dimension of sin: the sin against Bathsheba and Uriah is simultaneously and ultimately a sin against God. Not that David did not sin against humans, but that the divine offense is primary. lĕmaʿan tiṣdaq bĕdabbĕrekhā — so that you are proved right when you speak. Ṣādaq (qal: to be righteous, be in the right) when he speaks his verdict — the confession validates the divine judgment. Paul cites this in Romans 3:4 (hina an dikaiōthēs en tois logois sou: that you might be justified in your words) as establishing the universal sinfulness that only divine justification can address.
5: hēn bĕʿāwôn ḥôlāltî — surely I was brought forth into guilt. Ḥôlāltî (polal of ḥûl/ḥîl: to be brought forth in labor-pangs — to be born). ʿĀwôn (iniquity, guilt — the state of crooked relationship to God's standard). The Psalmist traces his present sin back to his condition at birth — solidarity with human sinfulness. ûbĕḥēṭ yĕḥemmatanî ʾimmî — and in sin my mother conceived me. Not a statement that conception or birth are sinful acts, but that the human condition from its first moment is marked by the sinful state of humanity that precedes any individual act.
6: hēn ʾĕmet ḥāpaṣtā battûḥôt — you desire truth deep inside a person. Battûḥôt (in the innermost parts — the hidden interior of a person; from tāḥaḥ: to be beneath/below). ʾĕmet (truth, reliability — the covenant faithfulness). God's requirement is not exterior performance but interior truthfulness. ûbĕsātûm ḥokhĕmāh tôdîʿēnî — in the hidden place you are teaching me wisdom. Sātûm (the covered, hidden place — the innermost secret; from sātar: to hide). The divine instruction in the hidden interior: wisdom taught in the secret place, corresponding to the truth required there.
7: tĕḥaṭṭĕʾênî bĕʾēzôb wĕʾeṭhār — cleanse me with hyssop, and I will be clean. ʾĒzôb (hyssop — the small, fragrant herb used in ritual purification for those with skin disease or corpse-impurity: Leviticus 14:4-7, 49-52; Numbers 19:6, 18). The hyssop dipped in the blood of a bird or the water of purification was used to sprinkle the unclean. Here the Psalmist applies the imagery to his inner moral condition. tĕkabbĕsênî ûmishsheleg ʾalbîn — wash me, and I will be whiter than snow. The comparison to snow (sheleg) sets the highest possible standard of whiteness — snow is the purest white in the natural world (cf. Isaiah 1:18: ʾim-yihyû ḥăṭāʾêkhem kashĕnî kĕsheleg yalbînû).
8: tashmiʿênî śāśôn wĕśimḥāh — let me hear the sounds of joy and gladness. The petition to hear joy and gladness as the divine response to forgiveness — the senses restored to their proper objects. tāgēlanāh ʿăṣāmôt dikkîtāh — let the bones you have crushed be glad. Dākhāh (piel: to crush — the same root as dakkāʾ in Psalms 90:3). The bones as the deepest physical structure — divine judgment reaches to the skeletal level. The crushed bones that are glad when forgiveness comes express the physicality of guilt and its relief.
9: hastēr pānêkhā mēḥăṭāʾāy — look away from my sins. Haster pānim (to hide the face — to look away from, not to see). The unusual request: the divine face hidden from the sins rather than from the sinner (normally, hester pānim from the person is the experience of abandonment; here it is requested that God look away from the sin record). wĕkhol-ʿăwōnōtay meḥē — wipe out all my guilt. Māḥāh again (cf. v1) — the complete erasure of all iniquity.
10: lēb ṭāhôr bĕrāʾ-lî ʾĕlōhîm — create a pure heart in me, O God. Bārāʾ (to create — the Genesis 1 creation word; used only of God, never of human creative activity). The request for a pure heart uses the creation vocabulary because moral renovation of this depth requires an act of new creation, not human moral improvement. The verb stands alone as the theological declaration: this is not possible through human effort. wĕrûaḥ nākhôn ḥadddēsh bĕqirbî — and renew a steadfast spirit within me. Ḥādash (piel: to renew — to make new again, to restore to original freshness). Rûaḥ nākhôn (a steadfast/established spirit — reliable, unwavering; from kûn: to be fixed, established).
11: ʾal-tashlîkhênî millĕphānêkhā — do not banish me from your presence. Shalakh millĕphānāyw (to cast from the face — the royal expulsion from the royal presence; cf. Cain's expulsion from YHWH's presence, Genesis 4:14). The theological horror of the petition: rejection from the divine presence is the ultimate punishment, worse than death. wĕrûaḥ qodshĕkhā ʾal-tiqqaḥ mimmennî — do not take your Holy Spirit from me. Rûaḥ qōdesh (Holy Spirit — the divine Spirit specifically designated as holy) — the petition not to have the Holy Spirit taken away reflects the experience of Saul (1 Samuel 16:14: wĕrûaḥ YHWH sārāh mēʿim-shāʾûl: the Spirit of YHWH departed from Saul) as the negative paradigm David fears repeating.
12: hāshîbāh lî śĕśôn yishʿekā — give back to me the joy of your rescue. Shûb (hiphil: to restore, return) — the joy of salvation that existed before the sin is asked to be restored to its prior state. wĕrûaḥ nĕdîbāh tisměkhênî — and sustain me with a generous, willing spirit. Rûaḥ nĕdîbāh (a generous/willing/noble spirit — from nādab: to volunteer, to give freely; the spirit of willing, ungrudging devotion). The spirit that gives freely to God without compulsion — the antithesis of the grudging, self-serving approach to worship criticized in Ps 50.
13: ʾălammĕdāh phōshĕʿîm dĕrākhêkhā — then I will show other rebels your ways. Pōshĕʿîm (the rebellious — the same root as pĕshāʿ in v1). The forgiven rebel becomes the teacher of other rebels — the missionary consequence of forgiveness. wĕḥaṭṭāʾîm ʾēlêkhā yāshûbû — and sinners will come back to you. Shûb (to return, repent) — the sinners will turn back to God through the testimony of the forgiven Psalmist. The repentance of the one generates the repentance of others.
14: haṣṣîlênî middāmîm ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhê tĕshûʿātî — rescue me from the guilt of bloodshed, O God — God of my salvation. Dāmîm (bloods — the plural of blood, used specifically of shed blood and its guilt; Uriah's blood). The specific sin named: bloodguilt, the most severe category of moral pollution in the OT (Numbers 35:33; Deuteronomy 19:10). tĕrannēn lĕshônî ṣidqātekhā — and my tongue will ring out your righteousness. The forgiven tongue — freed from the guilt that silenced it — sings the divine righteousness. The tongue that was the instrument of deception in David's sin becomes the instrument of praise.
15: ʾădōnāy śĕphātay tiphttāḥ — O Lord, open my lips. The paradox: the Psalmist cannot open his own lips to praise because sin has closed them. The divine action (opening lips) must precede the human act (praise). ûphî yaggîd tĕhillātekhā — and my mouth will declare your praise. The praise that follows the divine opening — not self-initiated but response to divine gift. The mouth that was loosened in evil speech (cf. Psalms 50:19) is now opened by God for praise.
16: kî lōʾ-taḥpōṣ zebaḥ wĕʾettēnāh — you do not want sacrifice — otherwise I would bring it. The echo of Psalms 50:8-14 — the theme of what God truly wants as sacrifice. The Psalmist will not bring animal sacrifice not because he refuses but because he understands God's actual desire. ʿôlāh lōʾ tirṣeh — you take no delight in burnt offerings. Rāṣāh (to be pleased with, to accept favorably — the technical term for the divine acceptance of a sacrifice). The ʿôlāh (burnt offering — the wholly burned offering) does not achieve what only contrition achieves.
17: zibĕḥê ʾĕlōhîm rûaḥ nishbārāh — God's desired sacrifice is a broken spirit. Rûaḥ nishbārāh (a broken spirit — from shābar: to break, shatter). The spirit broken by the weight of known guilt is the sacrifice that pleases God — a complete inversion of the sacrificial system's material expectations. lēb-nishbār wĕnidkkeh ʾĕlōhîm lōʾ tivzeh — a broken and crushed heart, O God, you will not despise. Nidkeh (niphal of dākhāh: crushed — the same root as dakkāʾ in Psalms 90:3 and dikkîtāh in Psalms 51:8). The divine verdict: the broken and crushed heart will not be despised (bāzāh: to treat with contempt). God who rejects the formally correct sacrifice accepts the broken heart.
18: hêṭîbāh birṣônĕkhā ʾet-ṣiyyôn — in your favor, do good to Zion. The communal petition — the individual psalm reaches outward to the community. tibneh ḥômôt yĕrûshālāim — build up the walls of Jerusalem. Bānāh ḥômôt (to build walls — the construction of the city's defenses). This verse (and v19) are often read as a later addition reflecting the exile when Jerusalem's walls were destroyed. Whether original or added, the movement from personal contrition (v17) to communal restoration (v18-19) is theologically significant: the individual's forgiveness is connected to the community's welfare.
19: ʾāz taḥpōṣ zibĕḥê-ṣedeq — then you will be pleased with right sacrifices. Zibĕḥê-ṣedeq (sacrifices of righteousness — sacrifices offered from a right heart, the inner condition that makes the outward offering acceptable; cf. Deuteronomy 33:19; Psalms 4:5). ʿôlāh wĕkhālîl — with burnt offerings and whole offerings. The animal sacrifices that v16 declared God did not want are not permanently abolished — they are restored to their proper function once the heart is right (v17). ʾāz yaʿălû ʿal-mizbĕḥakā phārîm — then bulls will go up on your altar. The material offerings return to their proper place — after, not instead of, the broken and contrite heart.
